results.title: Humalike: Give your AI agents the social intelligence they're missing | Product Hunt
results.content:
Report

Mateusz Jacniacki

Maker

@noctis06 Hey Noctis, these parts live on Humalike side. Both memory and classifier are on our side for few reasons:   
1. ease of integration   
2. easy way to ship improvements  
3. the Hermes plugin uses Humalike APIs that are transferable from product to product. That means if you ever wanted to create your own agent you can easily use Humalike APIs and get similar behavior as Hermes x Humalike  
  
Cost per turn is low from what we tested, when you sign up you get 10.000 free credits which should be enough for months of normal usage for hermes agent.  
  
Let me know if you are concerned about something else specifically:))

Report

Dipankar Sarkar [...] Hey PH 👋 Martí here, co-founder of Humalike.  
  
What is Humalike? The behavioral infrastructure for humanlike AI agents. The social skills your agents have been missing.  
  
Today we're shipping the fastest way to feel what that means: a plugin that makes your Hermes agent fit in 1-1's / groups.  
  
The problem  
We run Hermes agents in our own Slack and Telegram. Brilliant at tasks. Painful to use in groups and treat is as a companion. It answered every single message, talked over people, spammed 10 lines when one was enough. Everyone knew it was a bot instantly. That's not a model problem, it's a behavior problem.  
  
- What the plugin does: Decides, when to jump in and when to stay quiet  
- Paces replies like a human: typing speed, pauses, 1-3 short messages instead of a wall [...] Subscribe

results.title: Hermès International (French luxury manufacturer) is rated "Bad" with 1.4 / 5 on Trustpilot
results.content: Considering 37 reviews, most reviewers were let down by their experience overall. Many customers found the customer service to be poor, describing staff as unhelpful, arrogant, and lacking in urgency or accountability. People frequently encountered issues with product quality, noting items with visible scuffs, creases, or threads pulling off, which they felt was unacceptable for a luxury brand. Th e return policy was a major source of frustration, with many reporting difficulties returning items due to strict conditions, such as tiny marks on soles or removed plastic, leading to refusal of refunds or exchanges. Some people also felt that the company did not care about customers and that their craftsmanship was no longer reliable. However, a few other people also felt that the customer [...] I know you’re security, I was actually looking for that gentleman, as I point to a sales person. I have a discussion with the salesman to find my size and arrange a try on. He walks away to facilitate what we arrange. I began browsing again, and every turn I made the security guard was on my hills. I intentionally walked into one direction and pivoted to walk in a different direction, to my dismay, he was once again right on my heels. It’s so disappointing to have such an experience; I walked out without even trying anything on or buying a thing. I usually shop in NYC, and have shopped in the Chicago store. Never have I had such a humiliating and frustrating experience. Needless to say I’ll never ever go back into the Toronto store. [...] As a loyal customer of Hermes, it’s so disheartening to walk into one of their locations and be blatantly followed. I walked into the Toronto location, just killing time before my facial, I had a couple of bags with me. Wanting to not have such an experience, I asked if I could check my bags. I was told, unfortunately, they had no where to store them. Totally fine, I browse downstairs a bit and proceed upstairs. I could see, from my periphery, a security personnel follow me up the stairs; and thinking it’s nothing, I continue to browse. I came across a shirt, sweater and pair of pants I like. Immediately as I turn around, a security guard is behind me. He tells me, “Oh I’m security, you’ll have to check in downstairs”. I respond I know you’re security, I was actually looking for that

results.title: Am I using Hermes wrong, or is it genuinely not as smart as ...
results.content: Hermes was the idea that I could gradually build a system that automates lesson planning using long-term memory. In theory, it sounded perfect. In practice, it’s been a big headache. I haven’t managed to accomplish anything with Hermes that I couldn’t have done faster and more reliably with Claude or ChatGPT. I also set up an Obsidian Vault on day one as a structured long-term memory layer. At first it worked reasonably well, but over time Hermes started misfiling things, forgetting to store important info, and—most frustratingly—not checking the Vault unless explicitly told to. If I have to remind it to use its own memory, what’s the point? To address the gap between short-term and long-term memory, I installed Mnemosyne. That hasn’t helped at all. Hermes doesn’t maintain it unless I [...] hasn’t helped at all. Hermes doesn’t maintain it unless I prompt it repeatedly, and even then it admits it “dropped the ball” and then proceeds to do the same thing again later. I’ve also tested multiple models (DeepSeek V4 Flash, Owl Alpha, Mimo v2.5, Minimax M3), and the issues seem consistent across all of them to varying degrees. So at this point I don’t think it’s just a model problem. Right now, using Hermes feels like more effort than it’s worth. I can see the potential, and I’ve seen others get great results, but my experience has been constant babysitting, troubleshooting, and frustration. Am I missing something fundamental in how Hermes is supposed to be set up or used, or is this just the ACTUAL current reality of these “memory-driven” agents? [...] - The onething i found out is using the commands better. Even with chat. The commands is where you make the best out of it. Every new session it just like a complete wipe and if you use different model each time the results will be unpredictable. If you build a skill using one model the other model doesnt really read it like the other models. Even if your adjusting the reasoning level. The effect is not the same. Its kinda clunky. And you must now anything and help the agent set things up. Ip it is already set then its magical.

results.title: Hermes plugin | Humalike | Humalike
results.content:
Humalike × Nous Research

HumalikeGitHub

# Make your Hermes Agent feel more Humanlike.

```
hermes update && git clone  ~/.hermes/plugins/humalike && hermes plugins enable humalike && hermes
```

View on GitHub

Engraved many-armed Hermes figure

One shot integrations:

TelegramSlackWhatsAppDiscordTelegramSlackWhatsAppDiscord

#1  Turn-taking

### Knows when to speak

Knows when to speak

Reads the chat’s rhythm and decides whether to jump in or stay silent. No wall of bot text.

#2  Persona

### Has a self

Has a self

/soul enhance turns a one-line description into a fully-voiced character, so replies sound like someone.

#3  Theory of mind

### Reads the room

Reads the room

Weighs how a message will land for the person on the other end, and adjusts before sending. [...] #4  Social learning

### Adapts to group's tone

Adapts to group's tone

Picks up the group’s slang, formatting, and formality, and starts sounding like a member.

## Feels like a person. Everywhere it talks.

View on GitHub
